text: The surgeon begins dissection by opening the peritoneal leaf of the transverse mesocolon medially to the triads. Due to the absence of the left branch of the middle colic artery, the mesocolon is dissected from the body and tail of the pancreas, revealing the epiploon retrocavity. The vascular dissection exposes the left colic vessels, which are sealed using a radiofrequency device at the confluence of the left colic vein with the inferior mesenteric vein, without clips or sutures. The dissection continues in the left retrocollic space along Toll's fascia, proceeding from medial to lateral.

The surgeon separates the transverse mesocolon from the tris, facilitating the mobilization of the medial transverse colon for anastomosis. The left colon is cleaved from the left periotocolic space, progressing from bottom to top over the splenic flexure.

The surgeon completes the mesocolon dissection and sections the descending and transverse colon using a linear stapler. Following the resection of the specimen, a primary anastomosis is performed with a completely intracorporeal side-to-side stapled isoperistaltic anastomosis between the transverse and descending colon.
